meta.title: Mastering Endocrine & Diabetes (review-only; across 3a–3f with prior-module crossovers)
meta.note: Mastering subsections are review-only and should be appended after the app auto-builds the mastery pool. This pack is designed to be integrated the same way as prior mastery add-ons.

questions.questionText: You are reviewing basal-versus-bolus insulin products before clinic. Which brand/generic pair is correctly matched for a long-acting basal insulin?
questions.explanation: Lantus is insulin glargine, a long-acting basal insulin. Humalog is lispro, NovoLog is aspart, Levemir is detemir, and Humulin R is regular insulin.

questions.questionText: A patient uses NovoLog for mealtime coverage and asks when to inject it. Because insulin aspart is a rapid-acting bolus insulin, when is it generally given?
options: Immediately before a meal, usually within 5 to 10 minutes of eating
Only after the meal is completely finished
Once weekly on the same day each week
questions.correctAnswer: Immediately before a meal, usually within 5 to 10 minutes of eating
questions.explanation: Insulin aspart is rapid acting and is generally given within 5 to 10 minutes before a meal for prandial glucose control.

questions.questionText: One insulin from this module has a special high-yield pearl: it is the classic insulin used intravenously for emergencies like DKA and shifting potassium in hyperkalemia. Which insulin is it?
questions.explanation: Regular insulin is the classic short-acting insulin used intravenously in DKA and in insulin/dextrose protocols for hyperkalemia.

questions.questionText: A student is drawing up two insulins into the same syringe and asks whether Lantus can be mixed with other insulins. Which answer is most accurate?
options: No — insulin glargine should not be mixed with other insulins
Yes — glargine is routinely mixed with rapid-acting insulins
Yes — glargine should always be mixed with regular insulin
Yes — but only if the patient has type 1 diabetes
Yes — as long as the total dose stays under 10 units
questions.correctAnswer: No — insulin glargine should not be mixed with other insulins
questions.explanation: Insulin glargine is a long-acting basal insulin and should not be mixed with other insulins in the same syringe.

questions.questionText: Two patients need basal insulin. One is prescribed insulin detemir and the other insulin glargine. Which pearl best distinguishes detemir from glargine?
options: Detemir may require twice-daily dosing in some patients
Detemir is the preferred IV insulin in DKA
Detemir is a rapid-acting mealtime insulin
Detemir must always be taken with food
Detemir is contraindicated in type 2 diabetes
questions.correctAnswer: Detemir may require twice-daily dosing in some patients
questions.explanation: Insulin detemir is a long-acting basal insulin, but some patients require twice-daily dosing, unlike glargine which is more often once daily.

questions.questionText: Humalog is a rapid-acting insulin used for prandial coverage around meals. What is the generic name of Humalog?
questions.explanation: Humalog is the brand name for insulin lispro, a rapid-acting mealtime insulin.

questions.questionText: Whether the insulin is basal or bolus, the most important adverse effect students must recognize quickly is low blood sugar. Which adverse effect is shared across essentially all insulin products in this module?
questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia is the major class-wide adverse effect of insulin therapy and is especially important when meals are missed or doses are excessive.

questions.questionText: A patient injects insulin into the exact same abdominal spot every day and starts having erratic absorption. Which counseling point best teaches the prevention strategy?
options: Rotate injection sites to reduce lipodystrophy and absorption problems
Massage the same site after every injection to improve uptake
Switch all insulin doses to bedtime
Take the insulin only with a high-fat meal
Store the current pen at room temperature for several months
questions.correctAnswer: Rotate injection sites to reduce lipodystrophy and absorption problems
questions.explanation: Repeated injections into the same area can cause lipodystrophy and unpredictable absorption. Rotation of injection sites is an important insulin-counseling pearl.

questions.questionText: A newly diagnosed patient with type 2 diabetes needs a first-line oral drug that lowers hepatic glucose production and usually does not cause hypoglycemia when used alone. Which drug best fits that description?
questions.explanation: Metformin is the classic first-line oral agent for type 2 diabetes. It decreases hepatic glucose production and generally does not cause hypoglycemia when used alone.

questions.questionText: A patient starts Glucophage and develops nausea and loose stools. Which counseling point is most likely to improve tolerability while keeping the patient on this high-yield first-line drug?
options: Take metformin with meals and titrate gradually
Take it on an empty stomach with calcium
Stop all food intake for two hours after each dose
Switch the dose to immediately before bedtime on day one
Take it only when fasting glucose is above goal
questions.correctAnswer: Take metformin with meals and titrate gradually
questions.explanation: GI upset is common when metformin is started. Taking it with meals and increasing the dose gradually can improve tolerability.

questions.questionText: Metformin is high yield not just for its mechanism, but also for a long-term deficiency it can cause. Which lab issue becomes important to monitor over time in a patient on chronic metformin therapy?
questions.explanation: Long-term metformin therapy can lower vitamin B12 levels, so B12 deficiency is an important monitoring pearl in chronic use.

questions.questionText: A patient on metformin is scheduled for a contrast-enhanced CT scan and has borderline renal function. Which medication is classically held around this scenario because of lactic acidosis concern if kidney function worsens?
questions.explanation: Metformin is commonly held around iodinated contrast exposure in patients at risk for renal injury because reduced clearance can increase lactic acidosis risk.

questions.questionText: Actos improves insulin sensitivity rather than directly stimulating pancreatic insulin release. Through which high-yield mechanism does pioglitazone primarily work?
options: SGLT2 blockade in the proximal tubule
ATP-sensitive potassium channel closure on beta cells
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone is a thiazolidinedione that improves insulin sensitivity through PPAR-gamma agonism.

questions.questionText: A patient with type 2 diabetes starts Actos and soon develops worsening ankle edema and rapid weight gain. Which major caution makes pioglitazone a poor choice in patients with significant heart failure?
options: It can cause fluid retention and worsen heart failure
It commonly causes severe hypoglycemia even when used alone
It is directly nephrotoxic and usually causes acute tubular necrosis
It causes agranulocytosis in a dose-dependent fashion
questions.correctAnswer: It can cause fluid retention and worsen heart failure
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone can cause fluid retention, edema, and worsening heart failure, which is why it is high yield to avoid or use very cautiously in patients with significant HF.

questions.questionText: Empagliflozin and dapagliflozin help lower glucose by acting in the kidney rather than the pancreas. What do SGLT2 inhibitors primarily block?
options: Renal glucose reabsorption in the proximal tubule
Peripheral conversion of T4 to T3
Aldosterone receptors in the collecting duct
questions.correctAnswer: Renal glucose reabsorption in the proximal tubule
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors block glucose reabsorption in the proximal renal tubule, causing glucosuria and lowering plasma glucose.

questions.questionText: A patient has type 2 diabetes plus heart failure and chronic kidney disease. Which drug class from this module is especially high yield because it can help all three problems?
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors are high yield because, beyond glucose lowering, they have important heart failure and kidney-protective roles.

questions.questionText: Jardiance is one of the highest-yield SGLT2 inhibitors because of its diabetes, heart-failure, and kidney benefits. What is the generic name of Jardiance?
questions.explanation: Jardiance is the brand name for empagliflozin.

questions.questionText: A patient taking a -gliflozin is scheduled for surgery. Because this class can contribute to euglycemic ketoacidosis around fasting and procedures, what is the usual high-yield perioperative counseling point?
options: Hold the SGLT2 inhibitor for at least 3 days before surgery when possible
Double the dose the day before surgery
Continue it but skip all insulin
Switch it to bedtime the night before surgery
Take it with a large carbohydrate load immediately before anesthesia
questions.correctAnswer: Hold the SGLT2 inhibitor for at least 3 days before surgery when possible
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors are commonly held for at least 3 days before surgery or prolonged fasting because of ketoacidosis risk.

questions.questionText: When counseling a patient starting Farxiga or Jardiance, which common class adverse effect should be discussed up front?
questions.explanation: Genital mycotic infections are a classic counseling point for SGLT2 inhibitors because they increase urinary glucose exposure.

questions.questionText: An older adult starts empagliflozin and later reports dizziness, orthostasis, and mild dehydration. Which class effect best explains these symptoms?
options: Osmotic diuresis leading to volume depletion
questions.correctAnswer: Osmotic diuresis leading to volume depletion
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors increase urinary glucose and water loss, so osmotic diuresis and volume depletion are important counseling points.

questions.questionText: Ozempic and Wegovy are two high-yield brand names tied to one very testable generic. What is the generic name of Ozempic/Wegovy?
questions.explanation: Ozempic and Wegovy are brand names for semaglutide.

questions.questionText: Two GLP-1 receptor agonists appear in this module. Which one is classically remembered as a once-daily injection rather than a once-weekly product?
questions.explanation: Liraglutide is the daily GLP-1 receptor agonist in this module, while semaglutide is the classic once-weekly GLP-1 option.

questions.questionText: A patient wants a diabetes medication that often helps with weight loss and slows gastric emptying while increasing glucose-dependent insulin secretion. Which drug class best fits that description?
questions.explanation: GLP-1 receptor agonists increase glucose-dependent insulin secretion, slow gastric emptying, and often promote weight loss.

questions.questionText: A patient with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma asks whether Ozempic is safe to use. Which answer is most appropriate?
options: No — semaglutide is contraindicated with a personal or family history of MTC or MEN2
Yes — semaglutide is preferred in MEN2 because it lowers weight
Yes — but only if taken with metformin
Yes — because the warning applies only to sitagliptin
No — but only because semaglutide causes severe hypoglycemia when used alone
questions.correctAnswer: No — semaglutide is contraindicated with a personal or family history of MTC or MEN2
questions.explanation: Semaglutide carries a thyroid C-cell tumor warning and is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of MTC or MEN2.

questions.questionText: Januvia is a weight-neutral DPP-4 inhibitor that rarely causes hypoglycemia by itself. What additional high-yield dosing pearl should students remember about sitagliptin?
options: It often needs renal dose adjustment
It must always be injected weekly
It should never be combined with metformin
It is mainly used for hyperthyroidism
It should be held because of bradykinin-mediated cough
questions.correctAnswer: It often needs renal dose adjustment
questions.explanation: Sitagliptin is a DPP-4 inhibitor with low hypoglycemia risk alone, but renal dose adjustment is a key prescribing pearl.

questions.questionText: A patient on sitagliptin develops blistering erosions, and the team is concerned about a rare but classic postmarketing warning. Which drug is most closely associated with bullous pemphigoid in this module?
questions.explanation: Bullous pemphigoid is a high-yield rare adverse effect associated with DPP-4 inhibitors such as sitagliptin.

questions.questionText: A patient takes glipizide and then skips breakfast, increasing the risk of symptomatic hypoglycemia. What counseling point was most likely missed?
options: Glipizide is typically taken about 30 minutes before a meal
Glipizide should only be taken once weekly
Glipizide should be taken at bedtime with no food
Glipizide is only for type 1 diabetes
Glipizide must be taken with calcium supplements
questions.correctAnswer: Glipizide is typically taken about 30 minutes before a meal
questions.explanation: Glipizide is commonly taken about 30 minutes before a meal to reduce postprandial glucose, so skipping that meal increases hypoglycemia risk.

questions.questionText: A student is comparing the two sulfonylureas in this module. Which drug is classically administered with breakfast or the first main meal of the day, often as once-daily therapy?
questions.explanation: Glimepiride is the once-daily sulfonylurea in this module and is administered with breakfast or the first main meal.

questions.questionText: A patient newly prescribed Synthroid asks how to take it correctly. Which counseling statement is most accurate and high yield?
options: Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach and separate it from calcium or iron by about 4 hours
Take levothyroxine only with a large breakfast to improve absorption
Take levothyroxine together with calcium for better thyroid replacement
Take levothyroxine only at bedtime with dairy products
Crush levothyroxine into antacid suspension before use
questions.correctAnswer: Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach and separate it from calcium or iron by about 4 hours
questions.explanation: Levothyroxine is best taken on an empty stomach, and calcium/iron can impair absorption, so they should be separated by about 4 hours.

questions.questionText: One thyroid hormone product in this module is synthetic T3 rather than T4 and therefore has a shorter half-life and faster onset. Which drug is it?
questions.explanation: Liothyronine is synthetic T3. Levothyroxine is synthetic T4 and has a longer half-life.

questions.questionText: A nonpregnant adult with Graves disease is starting drug therapy for hyperthyroidism. Which thionamide is usually preferred for most patients because it is generally favored over PTU?
questions.explanation: Methimazole is generally preferred over PTU for most nonpregnant patients with hyperthyroidism.

questions.questionText: Which antithyroid drug is classically preferred in the first trimester of pregnancy and is also remembered for blocking peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 in thyroid storm?
questions.explanation: PTU is high yield for two reasons: it is preferred in the first trimester and it also blocks peripheral conversion of T4 to T3, which is useful in thyroid storm.

questions.questionText: A patient taking methimazole calls the clinic reporting fever and a very sore throat. Which serious adverse effect must be considered urgently with thionamide therapy?
questions.explanation: Methimazole and PTU can rarely cause agranulocytosis. Fever or sore throat should prompt urgent evaluation.

questions.questionText: A euthyroid patient asks for thyroid hormone solely to lose weight faster. Which response best reflects a high-yield boxed-warning pearl for levothyroxine?
options: Thyroid hormone should not be used for weight loss in euthyroid patients
Levothyroxine is ideal for routine cosmetic weight loss
Levothyroxine should be used whenever BMI is above 30
Levothyroxine is safer for weight loss than GLP-1 agonists
Levothyroxine works only if taken with methimazole
questions.correctAnswer: Thyroid hormone should not be used for weight loss in euthyroid patients
questions.explanation: Levothyroxine carries a high-yield boxed-warning pearl: thyroid hormones should not be used for weight loss in euthyroid patients because toxicity can be serious.

questions.questionText: A levothyroxine dose was just adjusted today. Because thyroid hormone has a long half-life, when is the peak therapeutic effect of a given dose typically reached rather than judged after only a few days?
questions.explanation: Levothyroxine has a long half-life, so the peak therapeutic effect at a given dose is reached over weeks rather than days.

questions.questionText: A patient with adrenal insufficiency needs physiologic steroid replacement rather than simply an anti-inflammatory steroid burst. Which corticosteroid in this module is the classic replacement choice because it also has meaningful mineralocorticoid activity?
questions.explanation: Hydrocortisone is the classic corticosteroid used for physiologic adrenal replacement and has more salt-retaining (mineralocorticoid) activity than the others in this module.

questions.questionText: Which corticosteroid in this module is most classically remembered as very potent and having little to no significant mineralocorticoid activity?
questions.explanation: Dexamethasone is the most potent steroid in this module and has minimal clinically significant mineralocorticoid activity.

questions.questionText: Which corticosteroid is the classic oral outpatient "workhorse" in this module, often taken in the morning and associated with hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, and adrenal suppression with longer courses?
questions.explanation: Prednisone is the common oral outpatient corticosteroid associated with classic steroid adverse effects such as hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, and adrenal suppression.

questions.questionText: You are reviewing IV steroid names for acute inflammatory flares. Medrol and Solu-Medrol correspond to which generic corticosteroid?
questions.explanation: Medrol and Solu-Medrol are brand names for methylprednisolone.

questions.questionText: A patient has been taking systemic corticosteroids for several weeks. Why is tapering often necessary instead of abruptly stopping the drug?
options: To reduce the risk of HPA-axis suppression and secondary adrenal insufficiency after withdrawal
questions.correctAnswer: To reduce the risk of HPA-axis suppression and secondary adrenal insufficiency after withdrawal
questions.explanation: Longer courses of systemic corticosteroids can suppress the HPA axis. Gradual tapering helps reduce the risk of steroid-withdrawal adrenal insufficiency.

questions.questionText: A patient is receiving immunosuppressive doses of prednisone and asks about live vaccines. Which teaching point is most accurate?
options: Live or live-attenuated vaccines are generally contraindicated at immunosuppressive steroid doses
Live vaccines are preferred because steroids improve vaccine response
All vaccines are ineffective once any steroid is started
Vaccines are only an issue with hydrocortisone, not prednisone
Prednisone must be stopped for one full year before any vaccine
questions.correctAnswer: Live or live-attenuated vaccines are generally contraindicated at immunosuppressive steroid doses
questions.explanation: At immunosuppressive corticosteroid doses, live or live-attenuated vaccines are generally contraindicated. Inactivated vaccines may be used, but response can be blunted.

questions.questionText: A patient stable on warfarin starts levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. Which monitoring test deserves closer follow-up because thyroid replacement can alter anticoagulant response?
questions.explanation: Levothyroxine can alter warfarin response, so INR monitoring is a high-yield cross-module interaction pearl.

questions.questionText: A patient uses insulin lispro for meals and propranolol for migraine prevention. Which counseling point is most important about this cross-module combination?
options: Propranolol can mask adrenergic warning signs of hypoglycemia
Lispro should never be used in patients with migraine
Propranolol converts lispro into a long-acting insulin
questions.correctAnswer: Propranolol can mask adrenergic warning signs of hypoglycemia
questions.explanation: Nonselective beta blockers such as propranolol can blunt adrenergic warning symptoms of hypoglycemia, making insulin therapy riskier to recognize early.

questions.questionText: A patient with heart failure is already taking furosemide and then starts empagliflozin for diabetes plus cardiorenal benefit. Which adverse-effect risk becomes especially important because both therapies can promote fluid loss?
questions.explanation: Furosemide and SGLT2 inhibitors can both contribute to fluid loss, so dizziness, orthostasis, and volume depletion are important cross-module counseling points.

questions.questionText: A patient takes low-dose aspirin for secondary cardiovascular prevention and then receives a longer course of prednisone. Which complication deserves extra counseling because this combination can increase risk?
questions.explanation: Aspirin plus systemic corticosteroids can increase GI irritation and bleeding risk, so patients should be counseled to report melena, hematemesis, or persistent epigastric pain.
